mechanism: Inhibits tyrosinase; disrupts melanosome formation; selectively targets hyperactive melanocytes

studies.title: Efficacy of hydroquinone in treating melasma: systematic review
studies.findings: Gold standard for melasma; 2-4% effective; 4% superior to 2%

studies.title: Hydroquinone safety and efficacy: review
studies.findings: Effective but concerns about exogenous ochronosis with prolonged use >6 months

notes: Gold standard but controversial. Limit to 3-6 months. Higher concentrations require prescription.

mechanism: Inhibits tyrosinase; reduces melanin production; antioxidant; brightens existing pigment

studies.title: Vitamin C and melasma: clinical study
studies.findings: 15-20% L-ascorbic acid significantly improved melasma and PIH

notes: Best in combination with vitamin E and ferulic acid. Unstable; formulation matters.

mechanism: Inhibits melanosome transfer from melanocytes to keratinocytes

studies.title: Topical niacinamide for hyperpigmentation: clinical study
studies.findings: 5% niacinamide significantly reduced hyperpigmentation and increased skin lightness after 8 weeks

notes: Excellent for all skin types. Very well tolerated. Good for PIH prevention.

mechanism: Inhibits tyrosinase; selectively targets abnormal melanocytes; anti-inflammatory

studies.title: Azelaic acid for melasma: systematic review
studies.findings: 20% azelaic acid comparable to 4% hydroquinone for melasma with fewer side effects

notes: Excellent alternative to hydroquinone. Good for acne-prone skin.

mechanism: Chelates copper in tyrosinase; inhibits melanin production

studies.title: Kojic acid and hydroquinone combination for melasma
studies.findings: Effective when combined; can cause irritation and contact dermatitis as monotherapy

notes: Often combined with hydroquinone or arbutin. Can be irritating and unstable.

mechanism: Plasmin inhibitor; reduces prostaglandin and arachidonic acid; decreases melanocyte-keratinocyte interaction

studies.title: Tranexamic acid for melasma: systematic review and meta-analysis
studies.findings: Significant improvement in melasma; topical 2-5% and oral 250-500mg both effective

notes: Excellent for melasma. Oral form requires medical supervision. Topical is safe OTC.

mechanism: Accelerates cell turnover; disperses melanin; inhibits tyrosinase transcription

studies.title: Tretinoin for melasma: Cochrane review
studies.findings: Effective for melasma and PIH; requires 24+ weeks for optimal results

notes: Slow results but very effective. Often combined with hydroquinone (Kligman's formula).

mechanism: Slow-release derivative of hydroquinone; inhibits tyrosinase without cytotoxicity

studies.title: Alpha arbutin as a skin-lightening agent: review
studies.findings: Effective at 1-2%; safer profile than hydroquinone; limited long-term RCTs

notes: Safer alternative to hydroquinone. Good for maintenance after hydroquinone treatment.

mechanism: Glabridin inhibits tyrosinase; liquiritin disperses melanin; anti-inflammatory

studies.title: Licorice extract for hyperpigmentation: clinical study
studies.findings: Showed skin-lightening effects; good tolerability; limited independent large-scale trials

notes: Gentle option for sensitive skin. Works well in combination with other brighteners.

mechanism: Accelerates cell turnover; removes pigmented keratinocytes; disperses melanin

studies.title: Glycolic acid peels for hyperpigmentation: clinical trial
studies.findings: Effective for PIH and melasma; higher concentrations more effective but more irritating

notes: Good for PIH. Often combined with retinoids or hydroquinone. Sun protection essential.

mechanism: Larger molecule AHA; slower penetration; exfoliates and brightens with less irritation

studies.title: Limited melasma-specific RCTs; primarily used for general brightening
studies.findings: Effective for skin brightening; lower irritation makes it suitable for darker skin tones

notes: Excellent for darker skin tones due to low irritation risk.

mechanism: Retinol-like activity; accelerates cell turnover; may inhibit melanin production

studies.title: Bakuchiol vs retinol for hyperpigmentation: clinical study
studies.findings: Reduced hyperpigmentation comparable to retinol with less irritation

notes: Good pregnancy-safe option for PIH. Emerging evidence.

mechanism: Antioxidant; photoprotective; anti-inflammatory; supports skin barrier

studies.title: Vitamin C and E combination for photoprotection
studies.findings: Synergistic with vitamin C; enhanced photoprotection

notes: Best used in combination. Not effective as monotherapy for hyperpigmentation.

mechanism: Tyrosinase inhibitor; antioxidant; anti-inflammatory

studies.title: Resveratrol as a tyrosinase inhibitor: in vitro study
studies.findings: Inhibits tyrosinase in vitro; limited clinical trials for hyperpigmentation

notes: Limited clinical evidence. Better as adjunct antioxidant.

mechanism: Stabilizes vitamins C and E; enhances photoprotection; antioxidant synergy

studies.title: CEF formula photoprotection study
studies.findings: Enhances vitamin C stability and efficacy

notes: Not a primary brightening agent. Essential for vitamin C formulations.

mechanism: Antioxidant; switches melanin synthesis from eumelanin to pheomelanin

studies.title: Glutathione for skin lightening: systematic review
studies.findings: Limited evidence; some studies show modest skin lightening effects

notes: Controversial. Limited and mixed evidence. Oral form more studied than topical.

mechanism: Inhibits tyrosinase glycosylation; reduces melanin production; promotes cell turnover

studies.title: Limited independent RCTs; primarily in combination products
studies.findings: Often combined with niacinamide; promising preliminary data

notes: Often paired with niacinamide. Gentle option.

mechanism: Inhibits tyrosinase; antioxidant

studies.title: In vitro data only
studies.findings: Shows tyrosinase inhibition in vitro; limited clinical trials

notes: Limited evidence. Natural alternative with good safety profile.

mechanism: Natural form of arbutin; releases hydroquinone slowly; inhibits tyrosinase

studies.title: Limited clinical data; traditional use
studies.findings: Traditional skin-lightening agent; limited modern clinical validation

notes: Natural alternative to synthetic hydroquinone. Less potent.

mechanism: Antioxidant; anti-inflammatory; inhibits melanin production

studies.title: Pycnogenol for melasma: clinical study
studies.findings: Showed reduction in melasma area; limited independent large-scale trials

notes: Limited evidence. Better as adjunct antioxidant.
